metadata.title: Digo (Wadigo/Adigo) Settlements and Areas
metadata.description: Comprehensive data on Digo towns, settlements, and culturally significant areas in Kenya and Tanzania for an interactive map

totalDigoPopulation.note: Kenya figure from 2019 KPHC; Tanzania figure is a projection from older census data

regions.name: Kwale County
regions.description: Primary homeland of the Digo people in Kenya. The Digo are the majority ethnic group in Msambweni, Lunga Lunga, and Matuga sub-counties.

subCounties.name: Kinango
subCounties.note: Duruma majority; Digo present in eastern parts

regions.name: Mombasa County (Likoni)
regions.description: Likoni sub-county in Mombasa is historically a Digo heartland, though now ethnically diverse.

regions.name: Mkinga District
regions.description: Primary Digo homeland in Tanzania. Digo and Segeju are the native ethnic groups, comprising the majority of the population alongside Sambaa settlers.

regions.name: Tanga City Outskirts
regions.description: Digo and Segeju were the original settlers of the area now comprising Tanga City. Small Digo communities persist in peri-urban wards.

settlements.name: Kwale
settlements.notable: County headquarters of Kwale County; gateway to Shimba Hills National Reserve. Administrative center for the Digo and Duruma communities. Site of county government offices, courts, and educational institutions.
settlements.digoSignificance: Main administrative town for the Digo people; Kwale is the primary town of the Digo and Duruma peoples

settlements.name: Ukunda
settlements.notable: Largest town in Kwale County. Serves as the commercial 'back office' for Diani Beach tourism. Home to Diani Airport (Ukunda Airstrip) with runway expansion completed 2025. Major retail chains, banks, and shopping centres have established presence. Main transport hub for south coast Kenya.
settlements.digoSignificance: Major Digo population center; gateway between Digo hinterland and coast

settlements.name: Diani Beach

population.note: Part of wider Ukunda-Diani urban area (~100,000)

settlements.notable: Voted Africa's leading beach destination multiple times. 10km stretch of white sand beach, 30km south of Mombasa. Home to luxury resorts, boutique hotels, and diving centres. Protected by a coral reef providing calm waters.
settlements.digoSignificance: Traditional Digo coastal territory; name derives from the Digo heritage of the area

settlements.name: Msambweni
settlements.notable: Fishing town with one of the world's largest continuous fringing reefs stretching north to Malindi. Home to Msambweni Level 5 Hospital (one of the best on the coast) and KMTC campus. Silica sand mined here is transported to Nairobi for glass production. Scenic beaches with fossilized coral formations and baobab trees.
settlements.digoSignificance: Heart of Digo fishing culture on the south coast

settlements.name: Lunga Lunga
settlements.notable: Kenya-Tanzania One-Stop Border Post, operational 24 hours. Located 102km south of Mombasa. Key trade corridor connecting Mombasa to Tanga and Dar es Salaam. The 6.5km gap between Lunga Lunga (Kenya) and Horohoro (Tanzania) border posts passes through lush tropical vegetation.
settlements.digoSignificance: Critical link between Kenya and Tanzania Digo communities; Digo people live on both sides of this border

settlements.name: Shimba Hills

population.note: National reserve; surrounding communities estimated at several thousand

settlements.notable: 300 km2 coastal rainforest reserve managed by KWS, 33km from Mombasa. Contains Kenya's only population of sable antelope and ~700 elephants. Over 50% of Kenya's 159 rare plant species are found here, including endangered cycads and orchids. Features Sheldrick Falls. Adjacent to Kwale town.
settlements.digoSignificance: Sacred hills in Digo cosmology; several Kaya sacred forests are located within and around the Shimba Hills range

settlements.name: Kinondo

population.note: Small village; part of wider Galu-Kinondo area

settlements.notable: Location of Kaya Kinondo, the primary sacred forest of the Digo people and a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The 30-acre ancient forest contains trees over 1,000 years old and 190+ tree species (140 classified as rare). Only Kaya forest open to visitors.
settlements.digoSignificance: Regarded as the original settlement (kaya) of the Digo people when they arrived at the coast ~600 years ago. Spiritual homeland of the Digo; also known as Kaya Ngalaani

settlements.name: Gazi
settlements.notable: Home to Mikoko Pamoja, the world's first community-based mangrove carbon credit project, winner of the 2017 Equator Prize. Gazi Bay is a critical mangrove ecosystem. About 90% of the population depends on fishing sustained by mangrove nurseries. Located 55km from Mombasa.
settlements.digoSignificance: Traditional Digo fishing village; pioneering community-led conservation by Digo women

settlements.name: Tiwi

population.note: Small settlement; specific census data not separately enumerated

settlements.notable: Quiet beach village 17km south of Mombasa, protected by coral reef with calm shallow waters. Less developed than Diani, retaining a secluded character. Part of Matuga Constituency. Has a sacred Kaya (Kaya Tiwi) nearby.
settlements.digoSignificance: Traditional Digo coastal settlement; site of Kaya Tiwi sacred forest

settlements.name: Shimoni
settlements.notable: Historic slave-holding port from the 1750s-1870s. Natural cave systems (Shimoni Caves) were used to hold enslaved people before transport. The British Imperial East Africa Company established its first headquarters here in the 1880s, building Kenya's first colonial prison. Today a tourist destination for cave tours, scuba diving, dolphin watching, and sport fishing in the Pemba Channel. Port of clearance for ocean-going dhows from the Persian Gulf and Zanzibar.
settlements.digoSignificance: Important port in Digo territory; mixed Digo-Swahili fishing community

settlements.name: Vanga
settlements.notable: Known as 'Kenya's last town' -- the southernmost coastal settlement before Tanzania. Famously has no bank, no church, no bar, and no guest house. A strategic fishing port with dozens of boats. 17km from Lunga Lunga border. Has deep historical ties to Arab and Shirazi traders. In the 19th century, Vanga's Muslim traders influenced Digo communities along the Umba River.
settlements.digoSignificance: Historic Digo trading center; Digo, Segeju, and Shirazi communities intermingled here for centuries. Key site in the Islamization of the Digo people

settlements.name: Matuga

population.note: Sub-county total; town itself is smaller

settlements.notable: Administrative center of Matuga sub-county. Located near Kwale town and Shimba Hills. The Digo are the majority ethnic group in this sub-county. Near Kaya Bombo sacred forest.
settlements.digoSignificance: Digo-majority sub-county center

settlements.name: Golini
settlements.notable: Highland village near Kwale town with views of Mombasa due to its elevated position. Important agricultural trading area for inland Digo farmers. Kwale town partially overlaps Golini sub-location.
settlements.digoSignificance: Inland Digo farming community

settlements.name: Bongwe
settlements.notable: Small Digo village near Ukunda. Situated along the road between Ukunda and Diani Beach area.
settlements.digoSignificance: Traditional Digo village community near the coast

settlements.name: Ramisi

population.note: Small town; specific census figure not separately available

settlements.notable: Site of Kenya's second sugar factory (established 1927), now the Kwale International Sugar Company. The Ramisi River flows nearby. Important agricultural area with sugar cane plantations in Digo territory. Historical center of sugar industry on the Kenya coast.
settlements.digoSignificance: Major Digo agricultural area; sugar industry historically provided employment for Digo communities

settlements.name: Likoni
settlements.notable: Historically the home of the Digo community in Mombasa, now a cosmopolitan melting pot. Connected to Mombasa Island by the Likoni Ferry. Home to Shelly Beach, the second-largest public beach in Kenya. Was a vibrant tourism and entertainment hub before security challenges. Gateway to Kwale County and the entire south coast.
settlements.digoSignificance: Northern boundary of traditional Digo territory; historically a Digo community before urbanization brought diverse settlers. The Digo name 'Likoni' persists

settlements.name: Wasini Island
settlements.notable: Island 3km off the coast opposite Shimoni, approximately 7km long and 3km wide. Two villages: Wasini and Mkwiro. Tourism is the main income source (50% of Wasini village depends on it), followed by fishing. Part of Kisite-Mpunguti Marine National Park area. Accessible by boat from Shimoni.
settlements.digoSignificance: Digo-Swahili island community; traditional fishing grounds of Digo fishermen

settlements.name: Horohoro
settlements.notable: Tanzanian side of the Lunga Lunga-Horohoro One-Stop Border Post. Located on trunk road T13 connecting Tanga to the Kenyan border. 6.5km from the Kenyan border post at Lunga Lunga. Key crossing point for Digo communities moving between Kenya and Tanzania.
settlements.digoSignificance: Border crossing linking Digo communities in both countries; Digo on the Tanzanian side transition into Mkinga District

settlements.name: Moa
settlements.notable: Ancestral home of the Digo and Segeju peoples on the Tanzanian coast. Ward in Mkinga District comprising 16 neighborhoods. Has a police station, health center (Moa Health Center), and several schools. Located on the Pemba Channel coast.
settlements.digoSignificance: Described as 'the ancestral home of the Digo people' on the Tanzanian side

settlements.name: Boma
settlements.notable: Small Digo ward south of Moa in Mkinga District.
settlements.digoSignificance: Traditional Digo fishing community

settlements.name: Mayomboni

population.note: Part of Mkinga District's 21 fishing communities

settlements.notable: Fishing community ward in Mkinga District, north of Moa.
settlements.digoSignificance: Digo fishing ward

settlements.name: Kwale (Tanzania)

population.note: Ward in Mkinga District; not to be confused with Kwale County, Kenya

settlements.notable: Shares the same name as Kwale County in Kenya, reflecting shared Digo heritage across the border.
settlements.digoSignificance: The shared name 'Kwale' on both sides of the border reflects the unity of the Digo people

settlements.name: Manza

population.note: Fishing community ward in Mkinga District

settlements.notable: Coastal fishing community south of Moa.
settlements.digoSignificance: Digo-Segeju fishing community

settlements.name: Tongoni (Mtangata)
settlements.notable: Site of the Tongoni Ruins, featuring ~40 standing tombs and a Friday mosque from the Swahili 'Golden Age' (1250-1550 CE). Vasco da Gama visited in April 1498. Located 21km south of Tanga City along the Tanga-Pangani route, overlooking Mtangata Bay. Mangrove-lined shore. Residents include Digo, Zigua, and Nyamwezi peoples.
settlements.digoSignificance: Historical Digo and Swahili settlement; Digo residents continue traditional fishing and farming

settlements.name: Parungu Kasera
settlements.notable: Administrative capital of Mkinga District. Located inland from the coast, serving as the government center for the predominantly Digo-Segeju district.
settlements.digoSignificance: Administrative center governing Tanzania's main Digo homeland

landmarks.name: Kaya Kinondo
landmarks.description: The primary Kaya of the Digo people and a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Contains trees over 1,000 years old, 190+ tree species (140 rare), and 5 possible endemic species. Active sacred site (mafingo) with 4 traditional paths, an old water hole, and a central clearing used for ceremonies. The only Kaya open to visitors as an eco-tourism site.
landmarks.digoSignificance: Spiritual homeland; believed to be the original fortified settlement of the Digo when they arrived at the coast ~600 years ago

landmarks.name: Kaya Bombo
landmarks.description: Secondary Kaya of the Digo Community. Located near Tangila and Manjera areas in the coastal hinterland. Part of over 50 identified makaya in Kwale County.
landmarks.digoSignificance: Sacred grove maintained by Digo elders; used for traditional ceremonies and spiritual practices

landmarks.name: Kaya Kwale
landmarks.description: Sacred Kaya forest near Kwale town, part of the network of over 50 makaya identified in Kwale County. Associated with the Digo and Duruma Mijikenda sub-groups.
landmarks.digoSignificance: Part of the sacred landscape of the Shimba Hills hinterland

landmarks.name: Kaya Diani
landmarks.description: Sacred Kaya near Diani Beach area. One of the secondary Kayas of the Digo Community.
landmarks.digoSignificance: Sacred forest in the Diani beach hinterland

landmarks.name: Kaya Tiwi
landmarks.description: Sacred Kaya forest near Tiwi village, part of the Kwale County network of sacred groves.
landmarks.digoSignificance: Traditional sacred site for Tiwi-area Digo communities

landmarks.name: Kaya Galu
landmarks.description: Sacred Kaya near Galu beach area south of Diani.
landmarks.digoSignificance: Part of the Digo sacred landscape between Diani and Kinondo

landmarks.name: Kongo Mosque
landmarks.description: One of the oldest mosques on the entire East African coastline, dating to the 13th-14th century (some sources say 16th century). Built with local coral rag stone in traditional Swahili coastal architecture. Features barrel vaults and an early classic mihrab style. Nestled among ancient baobab trees near the Kongo River estuary. Originally known as the Diani Persian Masjid. Named after Swaddiq Kongo, buried at the site. One of a handful of coral mosques still in use in Kenya.
landmarks.digoSignificance: Testifies to early Islamic influence among coastal Digo communities; Arab and Persian traders interacted with Digo people here for centuries

landmarks.name: Shimoni Caves
landmarks.description: Natural coral limestone cave system used as a slave holding port from the 1750s to 1870s. Enslaved people from the East African interior were held in these caves before being shipped to Zanzibar and beyond. Now a museum and tourist site managed by the National Museums of Kenya.
landmarks.digoSignificance: Located in Digo territory; Digo people were among those affected by the East African slave trade

landmarks.name: Fort Jesus
landmarks.description: Portuguese fort built 1593-1596 on Mombasa Island. UNESCO World Heritage Site. Located on Digo Road in Mombasa's Old Town. While primarily associated with Swahili-Portuguese-Omani history, the fort sits at the northern edge of Digo territory and the Digo were among the coastal peoples who interacted with successive colonial powers at Mombasa.
landmarks.digoSignificance: Northern boundary marker of Digo territory; Digo Road in Mombasa is named after the Digo people

landmarks.name: Lunga Lunga - Horohoro Border Crossing
landmarks.description: Modern One-Stop Border Post connecting Kenya's Kwale County to Tanzania's Mkinga District. Open 24 hours. The 6.5km stretch between the two border posts passes through lush tropical landscape with coconut palms and small Digo villages. Critical infrastructure for cross-border trade between Mombasa and Tanga/Dar es Salaam.
landmarks.digoSignificance: The colonial border divided Digo territory in 1886 (Anglo-German Agreement). This crossing is the primary link allowing Digo families and communities to maintain connections across the international boundary.

primaryActivities: mining (titanium at Kwale, silica at Msambweni)

tanzania.historicalIndustry: Sisal estates dominated the Mkinga economy until collapse in the 1970s
tanzania.uniqueResource: Umba sapphires (world's only source) found in the Umba Game Controlled Area within Mkinga District

sources: UNESCO World Heritage Centre - Sacred Mijikenda Kaya Forests
